next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The calibration rig's contact surface had gone from cold to warm to tacky with Lena's sweat in the first two hours. By the fourth, the dampness against her back had stopped mattering. Overhead, the fluorescent panels hummed at a frequency the system would normally have filtered out, a constant sixty-hertz buzz. Yusuf had disabled her automatic sensory filtering before the procedure began, and now every ambient sound arrived untouched: the equipment drone, the click of his keyboard, the thin whistle of ventilation through the ceiling vents.

"Stage four," Yusuf said. He took off his glasses and cleaned them on the hem of his shirt. He always did that at transitions, as if each new stage deserved a cleared view of the monitors. "This one remaps the motor pattern library. It's the longest stage. Ninety minutes, maybe more."

"What will it feel like?"

"Honestly, I don't know exactly. The simulation modeled pressure behind the eyes and at the interface." He touched the base of his own skull, mirroring her port. "The link's going to run hot again. Same as before, just sustained. I'll watch thermal output the whole time."

"And if it gets too hot?"

"Then I pull the stage and we're left with a half-rewritten motor library." He glanced at the screen. "Let's not do that."

"Okay."

"Ready?"

"Yes."

Heat bloomed at the port, the familiar two-centimeter circle of metal and ceramic at the base of her skull. It followed the neural pathways like liquid finding channels. That was the closest description she had. Signal routes were not supposed to have temperature, but she...
open_hooks_to_carry: Yusuf presents the partial patch: benefits plus costs including severing the neural coaching feed
Margot argues against the patch to protect her feed access
Yusuf reveals Farah's condition—this creates suspicion rather than trust
Lena torn: the patch might stabilize her or lock in the damage

payoffs_to_land.description: Yusuf's personal stake is revealed to the group—his sister's illness drives his data collection, complicating trust

prev_tail_excerpt:
...the angle and stayed there. I was still catching up."

She was quiet for a long time. Their shoulders were still touching. The wind kept pressing at the glass.

"That's not what happens to me," she said. "I go missing. You don't. You feel it while it's happening."

"Yeah."

"I thought that would make tonight impossible."

"Did it?"

She took her time. He let her. The ceiling was still boring and his wrist was still aching and the woman beside him was present, right now, in a way he could feel.

"No," she said. "But I felt it."

He reached for her hand and found it on the blanket between them. His fingers closed around hers. The touch registered in the usual way, the warmth and pressure first, then the system's precise measurement of her hand's temperature and the force he was exerting and the distribution of contact across both their palms. He held on to the first signal. The rough one. The imprecise one. The one that was his.

"It's doing it again," she said.

He felt it. His fingers had redistributed around hers, grip micro-correcting to equalize pressure. He hadn't told them to.

"Yeah."

He started to loosen his hold. Lena turned her hand over and laced their fingers together before he could pull away. His grip kept making small, unwanted corrections inside hers. She didn't comment on them again. The diagnostic display stayed dark. The hallway light held its thin gold bar across the floor. Outside, wind moved against the window with the constancy of altitude, and the mountains were behind the glass in the dark.

They lay there awake, feeling each correction as it came.
secondary_character_beats: Jin-seo argues for Meridian's solution with genuine conviction—she sees Yusuf's alternative as reckless experimentation on people she is trying to protect
state_deltas_required: Competing solutions force allegiances; every person's real investment is exposed
The group formally fractures along lines of self-interest; no option is clean
